prompt.text: Centrosome movement, spindle formation, and nuclear envelope breakdown.

questions.explanation: Prophase I opens meiosis I with nuclear envelope breakdown, centrosome migration, and spindle assembly—the same early mitotic events, but here they prepare homologous chromosomes for pairing and crossing over.

prompt.text: Chromosomes condense progressively.

questions.explanation: Chromosomes progressively condense during prophase I so homologs can synapse, recombine, and later be sorted accurately. Condensation makes chromosomes visible and manageable for the spindle.

prompt.text: Homologous chromosomes pairs are aligned and chromosomal cross over occurs.

questions.explanation: Homologous chromosomes pair and exchange segments by crossing over during prophase I. This recombination shuffles alleles between maternal and paternal chromosomes before the first division.

prompt.text: Chiasmatas first appear.

questions.explanation: Chiasmata—the X-shaped junctions where homologs stay connected after crossover—first appear in prophase I. They mark recombination sites and help hold homolog pairs together until anaphase I.

prompt.text: Microtubules from opposite poles attach to the kinetochores, one at the center of each homolog.

questions.explanation: In prophase I, spindle microtubules attach to kinetochores on homologous chromosomes as pairs form. This attachment sets up alignment and separation of homologs, not sister chromatids, in meiosis I.

prompt.text: Pairs of homologous chromosomes are aligned at the metaphase plate.

questions.explanation: At metaphase I, homologous pairs (not individual chromatids) align at the metaphase plate. This arrangement is the prerequisite for homologs to be pulled to opposite poles in anaphase I.

prompt.text: Each homologous pair is aligned at the metaphase plate independent of the other pairs.

questions.explanation: Independent assortment occurs at metaphase I: each homolog pair orients randomly relative to every other pair. The alignment of one pair does not dictate how any other pair is oriented.

prompt.text: Both chromatids of one homolog are attached to kinetochore of their nearest centrosomal pole.

questions.explanation: At metaphase I, both sister chromatids of a homolog attach to microtubules from the same pole, so the homolog moves as a unit. Sister chromatids are not separated until meiosis II.

prompt.text: Breakdown of proteins along chromatid arms allows homologs to separate.

questions.explanation: In anaphase I, cohesin along chromatid arms is cleaved, releasing homologous chromosomes from each other. Cohesin at centromeres still holds sister chromatids together until anaphase II.

prompt.text: Homologs move to opposite poles, guided by spindle apparatus.

questions.explanation: Anaphase I is defined by homologous chromosomes moving to opposite spindle poles. This is the reduction division that halves the chromosome number while keeping each chromosome duplicated.

prompt.text: Sister chromatids are still joined at the centromere, causing them to move as a unit.

questions.explanation: During anaphase I, sister chromatids remain joined at their centromeres and travel together to the same pole. Only homologs separate—unlike mitotic or meiosis II anaphase, where sisters split.

prompt.text: At the start, each pole has a complete haploid set of duplicated chromosomes.

questions.explanation: After telophase I, each pole holds a haploid set of chromosomes, each still composed of two sister chromatids. The cell is haploid in chromosome number but not yet haploid in DNA content per chromatid.

prompt.text: Cytokinesis forms two haploid daughter cells.

questions.explanation: Telophase I and cytokinesis complete the first meiotic division, producing two haploid daughter cells. No DNA replication occurs before the second division begins.

prompt.text: Two haploid daughter cells have formed, but no chromosomal duplication occurs.

questions.explanation: The two cells entering meiosis II are haploid and contain unduplicated chromosomes only in the sense that no new S phase occurs between divisions; each chromosome still consists of two sister chromatids.

prompt.text: Spindle apparatus forms in a haploid cell.

questions.explanation: Prophase II begins in haploid cells with spindle reformation and nuclear envelope breakdown. Unlike prophase I, there is no homolog pairing or crossing over in the second division.

prompt.text: In the late stage, sister chromatids are moved via microtubules to the metaphase plate.

questions.explanation: Late prophase II includes spindle attachment and movement of individual chromosomes toward the metaphase plate. Meiosis II sorts sister chromatids, not homologous pairs.

prompt.text: Sister chromatids are positioned at the metaphase plate.

questions.explanation: At metaphase II, duplicated chromosomes align individually at the metaphase plate, with kinetochores of sister chromatids facing opposite poles. This resembles mitotic metaphase but occurs in haploid cells.

prompt.text: Breakdown of proteins holding sister chromatids together, allowing them to separate and move to opposite poles.

questions.explanation: In anaphase II, centromeric cohesin is cleaved, allowing sister chromatids to separate and move to opposite poles—mechanistically similar to anaphase of mitosis.

prompt.text: Each chromatid is now an independent chromosome in a haploid cell.

questions.explanation: Once sisters separate in anaphase II, each chromatid is an independent chromosome in a haploid nucleus. This completes the reduction of DNA to the gamete state.

prompt.text: Nuclei form and chromosomes begin condensing.

questions.explanation: Telophase II includes nuclear envelope reformation and chromosome decondensation as division ends. These events reverse the changes initiated in prophase II.

prompt.text: Following cytokinesis, 4 genetically distinct haploid cells have formed.

questions.explanation: After cytokinesis following meiosis II, four haploid, genetically distinct gametes exist—variation arising from crossing over in prophase I and independent assortment at metaphase I.

prompt.text: An ordered display of an individual’s complete set of chromosomes, arranged in homologous pairs from longest to shortest, is known as a(n) __.

questions.explanation: A karyotype is a standardized display of an individual's chromosomes arranged in homologous pairs by size and banding pattern. It reveals chromosome number and major structural abnormalities, unlike genotype or phenotype descriptions.

prompt.text: If a specialized somatic cell of a specific animal species contains a total of 38 chromosomes, how many chromosomes would be present in a mature sperm cell of the same individual?

questions.explanation: Gametes are haploid (n), containing half the chromosome number of somatic (2n) cells. If 2n = 38, then n = 19 chromosomes in a mature sperm cell.

prompt.text: T/F: A sperm can contain an X or a Y chromosome.

questions.explanation: Human sperm carry either an X or a Y sex chromosome (plus 22 autosomes), so fertilization determines whether the zygote is XX or XY. Eggs always contribute an X chromosome.

prompt.text: T/F: Chromosome count directly correlates to species complexity.

questions.explanation: Chromosome number does not correlate with organismal complexity—some ferns have hundreds of chromosomes while humans have 46. Gene content and regulation matter more than chromosome count alone.

prompt.text: When an egg and sperm fuse, the resulting cell is known as a(n) ________ and is ________.

questions.explanation: Fusion of haploid egg and sperm produces a diploid zygote, restoring the full chromosome complement of the species. Gametes are haploid; embryos develop after the zygote begins dividing.

prompt.text: T/F: Animals, plants, fungi, and protists all utilize meiosis.

questions.explanation: Sexual eukaryotes—including animals, plants, fungi, and many protists—use meiosis to produce haploid cells for fertilization. It is the conserved mechanism for genetic reduction and recombination.

prompt.text: T/F: Hapoloids can undergo meiosis.

questions.explanation: Standard meiosis requires a preceding DNA replication in a diploid cell; haploid cells cannot undergo typical meiosis without first becoming duplicated. Organisms with haploid life stages enter meiosis only from a diploid precursor such as a zygote.

prompt.text: The principle of independent assortment during meiosis states that the orientation of one homologous pair of chromosomes at the metaphase plate is __.

options: Identical to the orientation of all other pairs
Independent of the orientation of other homologous pairs
Determined by the location of the centrosomes
Dependent on whether the chromosomes are maternal or paternal
questions.answer: Independent of the orientation of other homologous pairs
questions.explanation: Mendel's principle of independent assortment reflects random orientation of each homolog pair at metaphase I. How one pair aligns is independent of how any other homolog pair aligns.
